behaviors.description: The review input is scoped to the lines that changed, not the full content of each modified file.
behaviors.justification: This tests the input preparation logic—specifically, whether the code correctly filters a PR diff to extract only changed lines (marked with +/- in GitHub diff format) rather than including context lines or the entire file content. This is pure text parsing with no external dependencies. A Unit test with a realistic diff string (including context lines, @@ markers, file paths) will catch filtering bugs. No mocking of the LLM or GitHub API is needed to verify the scoping logic itself.
behaviors.test_description: Create a Unit test that provides a realistic GitHub diff containing context lines, added lines, deleted lines, and @@ line-number markers. Run the code's diff-parsing logic and verify that only the changed lines (+ and - prefixed) are extracted, excluding context lines. Test edge cases like multi-file diffs, diffs with no changes in a file, and files with identical surrounding lines.

behaviors.description: Inline comments are attached to the correct positions in the diff view on the pull request.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is 'comment posted to wrong line number in the PR diff view.' To catch this, we must validate that the code correctly maps from 'changed line in file' to 'position in GitHub's diff format,' which follows specific rules (position numbering, which lines are commentable, valid range bounds). A Unit test of isolated position-calculation logic might miss API contract violations or edge cases where the position is computed correctly but violates GitHub's API constraints. An Integration test with a mocked GitHub API (that validates GitHub's position format and range) catches the full mapping plus format errors. Testing against real GitHub (System) would work but is unnecessary for catching the logic error.
behaviors.test_description: Create an Integration test that provides a realistic PR diff, runs the code to extract findings and compute their inline comment positions, and mocks the GitHub API to validate that each position conforms to GitHub's API contract (correct line numbering, valid file path, position within valid diff range). Verify that invalid positions are rejected and that the mapping correctly handles multi-file changes, additions-only diffs, and deletions-only diffs.

behaviors.description: The agent identifies at least one real defect in a pull request that contains a known introduced bug.
behaviors.justification: Whether the code identifies a defect depends entirely on the real LLM's reasoning and capabilities applied to the specific code context. Mocking the LLM to return 'defect found' hides the real failure mode: the LLM may not recognize the bug because the prompt is unclear, the context is insufficient, or the model lacks the reasoning ability. The only way to catch this failure is to run the real LLM on real code containing a known bug and verify it surfaces the defect. This requires Agentic-level infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Create an Agentic test that uses the real LLM with a PR diff containing a known introduced bug (e.g., missing null check, off-by-one error, race condition). Run the full analysis pipeline and verify that the LLM identifies at least one defect matching the known bug. Run the test multiple times or with different bug examples to account for LLM non-determinism and robustness.

behaviors.description: Every finding listed in the top-level summary corresponds to an inline comment that was also posted — no findings appear in the summary without a matching inline comment.
behaviors.justification: This test verifies consistency in the code's internal output logic: for each finding, there must be a corresponding inline comment post, and vice versa. This is a Unit-level concern about logical correctness, not about external dependencies. By mocking the GitHub API, we can capture the sequence of all posts (inline comments and summary) and verify that every finding in the summary has a matching inline comment and no orphaned findings exist. If the code forgets to post an inline comment for a finding, the mocked API calls will reveal this inconsistency.
behaviors.test_description: Create a Unit test that mocks the GitHub API to capture all posted comments and summaries. Run the code's analysis and posting logic, then verify that for each finding in the summary comment, there is a corresponding inline comment post, and vice versa. Include edge cases such as multiple findings at the same location, findings that span multiple lines, and empty summaries.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — reading the diff, posting inline comments, posting the summary comment — completes correctly for a real pull request without manual intervention.
behaviors.justification: This behavior requires orchestration across multiple steps, several of which are non-deterministic or external: reading the diff (deterministic), analyzing with the real LLM (Agentic—non-deterministic), posting to GitHub (System—external), and handling errors (orchestration). The core failure modes are: (1) LLM analysis fails or returns invalid format (Agentic), (2) GitHub API rejects due to wrong format (System), (3) sequence breaks due to orchestration failure (Workflow). The minimum infrastructure to catch the most likely failure is Agentic, because if the LLM analysis works correctly in context, the other steps are more likely to succeed. Testing against a real LLM with a realistic PR (possibly using a test/sandbox GitHub account) is the minimum needed to catch whether the full pipeline works end-to-end.
behaviors.test_description: Create an Agentic test that runs the full end-to-end sequence: read a realistic PR diff, invoke the real LLM for analysis, post inline comments and a summary comment to either a real test GitHub account or sandbox PR. Verify that all steps complete without manual intervention, error handling works for transient failures, comments appear at the correct positions, and the summary accurately reflects the inline comments. Run the test on realistic PRs with varying complexity (trivial code, complex logic, mixed findings).
